name: Itztlacoliuhqui
description: Itztlacoliuhqui, also known as Itztlacoliuhqui-Ixquimilli, is an Aztec deity associated with frost, ice, cold, darkness, obsidian, and punishment. His name translates to "Curved Obsidian Knife" or "Obsidian Knife of Coldness," reflecting his association with sharp, cold materials and the pain they can inflict. He is often depicted with a curved obsidian knife protruding from his head, symbolizing his sharp, cutting power. Itztlacoliuhqui is believed to be the god of inanimate objects, particularly stone, and is sometimes linked to blindness or eye diseases. His role often involves bringing destruction and suffering, particularly through frost and cold weather, which could devastate crops and cause widespread hardship. He is considered a transformation of Tlahuizcalpantecuhtli, the Lord of the Dawn, after Tlahuizcalpantecuhtli failed to wound Tonatiuh, the sun god, with an arrow. As punishment, Tonatiuh transformed him into Itztlacoliuhqui, a deity of cold and darkness. This transformation highlights the consequences of challenging the established order and the power of the sun. Sacrifices were sometimes offered to Itztlacoliuhqui to appease him and prevent frost or other cold-related disasters. He represents the harsh aspects of nature and the potential for destruction, reminding the Aztecs of the constant need to respect and appease the powerful forces that governed their world. He is a chilling reminder of the darker side of the natural world and the consequences of hubris.
subtitle: God of Frost, Ice, and Obsidian

keyMyths.title: Transformation of Tlahuizcalpantecuhtli
keyMyths.description: Tlahuizcalpantecuhtli attempted to wound Tonatiuh but failed. As punishment, Tonatiuh transformed him into Itztlacoliuhqui, the god of cold and obsidian.

keyMyths.title: Association with Frost
keyMyths.description: Itztlacoliuhqui is believed to be responsible for bringing frost and cold weather, which could devastate crops and cause hardship. Offerings were made to appease him.

keyMyths.title: God of Inanimate Objects
keyMyths.description: Itztlacoliuhqui is considered the god of inanimate objects, particularly stone, highlighting his connection to the material world and its potential for harm.
keyMyths.source: Handbook to Life in the Ancient Aztec World

sources.author: Sahagún, Bernardino de
sources.description: An ethnographic study of Aztec culture and religion.
